G1_Rationale: Faster detection limits blast radius but MTTD alone does not guarantee containment actions that mitigate impact.
G2_Rationale: MTTD directly measures how quickly threats are identified within continuous monitoring workflows making it the primary detection velocity indicator.
G3_Rationale: MTTD provides useful benchmarking data for audit but is not itself a compliance requirement within continuous monitoring.
G4_Rationale: MTTD reflects tooling and process speed rather than analyst cognitive skill so it does not meaningfully signal security awareness gaps.
G5_Rationale: Tracking MTTD within SIEM/IDS monitoring enables efficiency gains through process optimization and prioritization of alert channels.
G6_Rationale: Reducing MTTD minimizes the window during which assets are exposed to active threats across the monitored environment.
G7_Rationale: MTTD is an internal SOC speed metric and does not measure intelligence sharing or collaborative detection with external partners.
G8_Rationale: Low MTTD in continuous monitoring indicates mature sensor coverage and proactive threat visibility before adversaries achieve objectives.

G1_Rationale: Alert volume awareness helps resource allocation but high volume without quality filtering does not directly improve impact mitigation outcomes.
G2_Rationale: High alert volume in continuous monitoring does not equate to quality detection; it may mask true positives within noise and degrade detection velocity.
G3_Rationale: Alert volume provides operational context for audit reviews but is not itself a compliance indicator in continuous monitoring.
G4_Rationale: Persistently high alert volume masks underlying analyst skill gaps and creates an illusion of activity without demonstrating genuine security awareness.
G5_Rationale: Alert volume is a useful workload awareness measure but volume alone is a throughput indicator rather than an efficiency driver.
G6_Rationale: Alert volume reflects the breadth of monitoring coverage but does not directly correlate with the protection of specific assets.
G7_Rationale: Alert volume data can be shared across shifts and teams but it does not constitute meaningful threat intelligence collaboration.
G8_Rationale: High alert volume may indicate broad sensor deployment but without quality filtering it does not advance proactive defense posture.

G1_Rationale: Productivity metrics incentivize closing incidents quickly which can lead to premature closure and failure to contain true threats reducing impact mitigation.
G2_Rationale: Incidents-per-shift throughput incentivizes cherry-picking easy false positives over investing time in genuine complex detections harming detection velocity.
G3_Rationale: Analyst productivity provides operational data relevant for workforce planning audits but does not directly satisfy compliance requirements.
G4_Rationale: Productivity counts measure throughput not analyst competency or awareness quality so they provide limited signal on security awareness.
G5_Rationale: Analyst productivity is a standard operational efficiency measure providing throughput visibility for shift management.
G6_Rationale: Throughput metrics do not directly correlate with the protection of specific assets unless quality of closure is also measured.
G7_Rationale: Productivity per shift is an internal operational measure and does not facilitate inter-team or external intelligence collaboration.
G8_Rationale: Productivity throughput metrics do not reflect proactive detection investment and offer no signal on emerging threat anticipation.

G1_Rationale: Burned-out analysts make more errors during detection increasing the risk that threats persist and cause impact though the link is indirect.
G2_Rationale: Analyst burnout can degrade alertness and detection quality over time but is an indirect rather than primary detection velocity driver.
G3_Rationale: Burnout tracking is not a standard compliance metric but supports workforce governance and duty-of-care obligations.
G4_Rationale: Monitoring analyst health directly measures human capital sustainability and signals when awareness and cognitive performance are at risk.
G5_Rationale: Reducing analyst burnout sustains operational throughput and prevents attrition-driven efficiency losses in continuous monitoring.
G6_Rationale: Burnout metrics do not directly measure asset protection capabilities though degraded analyst performance can indirectly increase asset risk.
G7_Rationale: Burnout data is internally focused and does not contribute directly to threat intelligence sharing or collaborative defense activities.
G8_Rationale: Analyst burnout monitoring supports workforce resilience but does not directly generate proactive threat intelligence or defensive actions.

G1_Rationale: Acknowledging alerts quickly is a prerequisite for containment but MTTA alone does not determine whether impact is ultimately mitigated.
G2_Rationale: MTTA measures responsiveness speed at the triage layer but speed of acknowledgment does not guarantee accurate threat detection.
G3_Rationale: MTTA is a standard SLA metric frequently cited in compliance frameworks as a measurable service level for alert triage processes.
G4_Rationale: Fast acknowledgment speed does not reflect analyst skill in evaluating alert legitimacy and may reward reflexive rather than analytical behavior.
G5_Rationale: Low MTTA reduces idle time between alert generation and analyst engagement directly improving triage throughput and operational efficiency.
G6_Rationale: MTTA measures process speed not the quality of asset-protective decisions made during triage.
G7_Rationale: MTTA is an internal process metric that does not measure collaboration quality or intelligence sharing between SOC tiers.
G8_Rationale: MTTA reflects reactive responsiveness and does not advance anticipatory or proactive threat detection capabilities.

G1_Rationale: Faster triage enables quicker escalation of genuine threats contributing to impact limitation though MTTT itself is a process speed measure.
G2_Rationale: MTTT directly measures analyst efficiency in working through alert queues supporting detection velocity by reducing time-to-decision.
G3_Rationale: MTTT can be used as an SLA reference in compliance reporting though it is not a primary compliance requirement.
G4_Rationale: Triage speed does not capture the quality or reasoning of analyst decisions and therefore does not indicate security awareness depth.
G5_Rationale: Reducing MTTT improves shift throughput and reduces alert queue backlogs directly supporting operational efficiency.
G6_Rationale: MTTT measures process speed at triage and does not directly reflect the quality of asset protection decisions.
G7_Rationale: MTTT is an internal triage performance metric and does not measure intelligence sharing or inter-tier collaboration.
G8_Rationale: Optimizing for triage speed may create pressure to close alerts quickly without investing in the deep analysis required for proactive defense.

G1_Rationale: Lower FPR reduces noise and helps analysts focus on genuine threats improving the conditions for impact mitigation though not a direct control.
G2_Rationale: Reducing FPR improves signal-to-noise ratio but aggressive threshold tuning to minimize FPR can create false-negative blindspots that harm detection velocity.
G3_Rationale: FPR is not a standard compliance requirement but well-managed FPR supports the credibility of audit evidence and incident records.
G4_Rationale: Low FPR improves analyst signal quality but blind tuning can mask gaps in detection capability without improving actual analyst security awareness.
G5_Rationale: Reducing FPR lowers alert fatigue and allows analysts to focus cognitive effort on genuine threats improving operational efficiency.
G6_Rationale: FPR management improves detection quality in triage but does not directly govern the protection of specific organizational assets.
G7_Rationale: FPR is an internal tuning metric that does not facilitate external intelligence sharing or inter-team collaboration.
G8_Rationale: Aggressive FPR reduction through blind threshold loosening can create false-negative blindspots that undermine proactive defense posture.

G1_Rationale: Alert volume awareness supports resource allocation at the triage layer but high volume does not directly improve impact mitigation.

G5_Rationale: Alert volume is a useful workload measure for triage planning but does not independently drive operational efficiency improvements.
G6_Rationale: Volume of alerts processed does not correlate with the quality of decisions made to protect specific assets.
G7_Rationale: Alert volume data can inform shift handover discussions but does not represent substantive intelligence collaboration.
G8_Rationale: High alert volume during validation does not indicate proactive defense capability and may divert resources from threat-hunting activities.

G3_Rationale: Analyst productivity data informs workforce planning for audit purposes but does not satisfy triage-specific compliance requirements.
G4_Rationale: Productivity counts measure throughput volume not analytical quality or security awareness demonstrated during validation decisions.
G5_Rationale: Analyst productivity provides triage throughput visibility useful for operational efficiency benchmarking and shift planning.
G6_Rationale: Productivity throughput during triage does not directly reflect the quality of asset-protective investigation decisions.
G7_Rationale: Productivity metrics are internally focused and do not contribute to collaboration or intelligence sharing across SOC tiers.

G6_Rationale: Burnout metrics do not directly measure asset protection quality though degraded analyst performance in triage increases asset exposure risk.

G1_Rationale: Fast resolution limits blast radius and constrains ongoing threat impact though strict timers risk premature closure that leaves root causes unresolved.
G2_Rationale: Strict MTTR targets pressure analysts to close incidents prematurely which forces resolution before full threat scope is understood degrading detection quality.
G3_Rationale: MTTR is a primary SLA metric embedded in most compliance frameworks and directly supports audit evidence for incident resolution timeliness.
G4_Rationale: MTTR measures speed of resolution not the analytical depth or reasoning quality that reflects genuine security awareness.
G5_Rationale: Reducing MTTR improves incident queue throughput and reduces resource time-on-incident contributing directly to operational efficiency.
G6_Rationale: Faster resolution reduces the window during which organizational assets remain exposed to active or persisting threats.
G7_Rationale: MTTR is an internal resolution speed metric and does not directly measure collaborative engagement or intelligence sharing with external entities.
G8_Rationale: MTTR optimization focuses on reactive resolution speed and does not advance anticipatory or proactive threat detection capabilities.

G4_Rationale: High escalation rates signal that Tier 1 analysts lack the training and skill to resolve incidents independently indicating security awareness gaps.

G6_Rationale: Escalation routing affects which expertise is applied to asset protection but escalation rate itself does not directly measure protection outcomes.
G7_Rationale: Escalation rate reflects Tier 1 to Tier 2/3 communication and shows cross-tier collaboration activity though it does not measure collaboration quality.
G8_Rationale: Escalation rate measures reactive inter-tier communication and does not indicate proactive threat anticipation or intelligence-driven defense activity.

G8_Rationale: High alert volume during incident handling is reactive in nature and does not advance proactive defense intelligence or threat anticipation.
